expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to naming and writing formulas for binary ionic compounds with variable-charge metals (Type II), where the metal can form multiple different charges, requiring Roman numerals (Stock system) to specify which ion is present.

core_concept.principle: Type II binary ionic compounds contain metals with variable charges (most transition metals, some post-transition metals); the name must include a Roman numeral in parentheses to specify the metal's charge in that particular compound.
core_concept.explanation: Type II ionic compounds are more complex than Type I because the metal can form multiple different ions. For example, iron can form Fe²⁺ or Fe³⁺, copper can form Cu⁺ or Cu²⁺, and lead can form Pb²⁺ or Pb⁴⁺. Since the charge isn't predictable from the periodic table position, we must specify it in the name using Roman numerals (Stock system). For example, FeCl₂ is iron(II) chloride (Fe²⁺) while FeCl₃ is iron(III) chloride (Fe³⁺). The Roman numeral tells us which specific ion is present, enabling us to write the correct formula and distinguish between different compounds of the same elements. This system, developed by Alfred Stock, provides unambiguous naming for compounds with variable-charge metals.

image.alt: Diagram showing Type II ionic compound naming pattern with Roman numerals
image.caption: Type II ionic naming: metal name + (Roman numeral) + nonmetal root + -ide (Roman numeral specifies metal charge).

sections.section_title: Identifying Type II Metals: Variable-Charge Elements
sections.introduction: Before naming Type II ionic compounds, you must be able to identify which metals have variable charges. These metals can form multiple different ions, requiring Roman numerals to specify which one is present in a particular compound.

key_concept.definition: Type II metals are elements that can form multiple different ionic charges. Most transition metals (d-block) are Type II, plus some post-transition metals like Pb, Sn, and Bi. Their charges must be determined from the compound formula or specified with Roman numerals in the name.
key_concept.context: Unlike Type I metals (Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd) which have fixed charges, Type II metals can lose different numbers of electrons depending on the compound. This variability requires the Stock system (Roman numerals) to specify the charge in each compound.

classifications.type: Common Transition Metals with Variable Charges
classifications.value: Most d-block metals form multiple ions (Fe, Cu, Cr, Mn, Co, Ni, etc.)
classifications.behavior: These metals can lose different numbers of d-electrons, creating multiple stable ions. The specific ion depends on the compound and reaction conditions.

examples.explanation: Fe forms Fe²⁺ (iron(II), ferrous) and Fe³⁺ (iron(III), ferric). FeCl₂ = iron(II) chloride; FeCl₃ = iron(III) chloride. Different colors: Fe²⁺ compounds often green, Fe³⁺ compounds often yellow/brown.

image.alt: Iron(II) and Iron(III) compounds showing different colors
image.caption: Iron forms two common ions: Fe²⁺ (green compounds) and Fe³⁺ (yellow/brown compounds).

examples.explanation: Cu forms Cu⁺ (copper(I), cuprous) and Cu²⁺ (copper(II), cupric). Cu₂O = copper(I) oxide (red); CuO = copper(II) oxide (black). Cu²⁺ is more common in aqueous solutions.

image.alt: Copper(I) and Copper(II) compounds showing different colors
image.caption: Copper forms two common ions: Cu⁺ (red compounds) and Cu²⁺ (blue/black compounds).

examples.explanation: Cr forms Cr²⁺ (chromium(II)) and Cr³⁺ (chromium(III)). CrCl₂ = chromium(II) chloride (blue); CrCl₃ = chromium(III) chloride (green/violet). Cr³⁺ is more stable.

image.alt: Chromium(II) and Chromium(III) compounds
image.caption: Chromium forms Cr²⁺ and Cr³⁺ ions with different colors and stability.

examples.explanation: Mn forms Mn²⁺, Mn³⁺, Mn⁴⁺, Mn⁶⁺, Mn⁷⁺ (multiple oxidation states). MnCl₂ = manganese(II) chloride (pink); MnO₂ = manganese(IV) oxide (black, in batteries).

image.alt: Various manganese compounds showing different oxidation states
image.caption: Manganese has many oxidation states: +2, +3, +4, +6, +7.

classifications.value: Pb (+2, +4), Sn (+2, +4), Bi (+3, +5) - metals after the transition series
classifications.behavior: These p-block metals can lose different numbers of electrons; +2 state often more stable for heavier elements (inert pair effect).

examples.explanation: Pb forms Pb²⁺ (lead(II), plumbous) and Pb⁴⁺ (lead(IV), plumbic). PbO = lead(II) oxide (yellow); PbO₂ = lead(IV) oxide (brown, in car batteries). Pb²⁺ more common.

image.alt: Lead(II) and Lead(IV) compounds
image.caption: Lead forms Pb²⁺ (more common) and Pb⁴⁺ (in batteries) ions.

examples.explanation: Sn forms Sn²⁺ (tin(II), stannous) and Sn⁴⁺ (tin(IV), stannic). SnCl₂ = tin(II) chloride (reducing agent); SnCl₄ = tin(IV) chloride (fuming liquid).

image.alt: Tin(II) and Tin(IV) compounds
image.caption: Tin forms Sn²⁺ and Sn⁴⁺ ions with different properties.

classifications.value: Hg forms Hg₂²⁺ (mercury(I), unusual diatomic ion) and Hg²⁺ (mercury(II))
classifications.behavior: Mercury(I) is unique: two Hg atoms bonded together with +2 total charge. Mercury(II) is the more common form.

examples.explanation: Hg₂²⁺ (two Hg atoms bonded): Hg₂Cl₂ = mercury(I) chloride (calomel, white solid). The Hg-Hg bond makes this ion unique.

image.alt: Mercury(I) diatomic ion structure
image.caption: Mercury(I): Hg₂²⁺ is a diatomic ion with Hg-Hg bond.

examples.explanation: Hg²⁺ (single atom): HgCl₂ = mercury(II) chloride (corrosive sublimate). More common than mercury(I) in modern chemistry.

image.alt: Mercury(II) compounds
image.caption: Mercury(II): Hg²⁺ is the more common mercury ion.

principles.statement: The Roman numeral in a Type II name specifies the exact charge on the metal ion in that particular compound, not the number of atoms.
principles.application: iron(II) = Fe²⁺ (charge +2, not 2 atoms); copper(II) = Cu²⁺ (charge +2); lead(IV) = Pb⁴⁺ (charge +4).
principles.example: FeCl₂: iron(II) chloride means Fe²⁺ (Roman numeral = charge). FeCl₃: iron(III) chloride means Fe³⁺. Same metal, different charges, different compounds.
principles.analogy: Like an ID badge: the Roman numeral tells you 'which version' of the metal is present (Fe²⁺ vs. Fe³⁺), like different uniform colors for different roles.

image.alt: Diagram showing Roman numerals indicate charge not atom count
image.caption: Roman numerals specify charge: iron(II) = Fe²⁺, NOT 2 iron atoms.

principles.statement: In a neutral ionic compound, the metal charge can be calculated from the known anion charge(s) and the formula subscripts.
principles.application: From FeCl₂: 2 Cl⁻ = -2 total, so Fe must be +2 → iron(II). From FeCl₃: 3 Cl⁻ = -3 total, so Fe must be +3 → iron(III).
principles.example: Cu?O: O is always -2, so Cu must be +2 to balance → copper(II) oxide (CuO). Cu₂?O: 2 Cu + (-2) = 0, so each Cu = +1 → copper(I) oxide (Cu₂O).

image.alt: Flowchart showing how to determine metal charge from formula
image.caption: Charge determination: total negative charge determines total positive charge needed.

principles.statement: Type II naming follows the pattern: [metal name] (Roman numeral) [nonmetal root]-ide, with spaces around the Roman numeral in parentheses.
principles.application: Correct: iron(III) chloride, copper(II) oxide, lead(IV) oxide. Incorrect: iron III chloride (no parentheses), iron(3) chloride (Arabic not Roman).
principles.example: FeCl₃: iron (metal) + (III) (charge) + chlor (root) + ide (suffix) = iron(III) chloride. Format is standardized by IUPAC.

image.alt: Stock system naming format breakdown
image.caption: Stock system format: metal + (Roman numeral) + nonmetal-ide.

components.definition: Type II metal identification and Roman numeral usage
components.equation: $\text{Metal Charge} = \dfrac{\text{Total Anion Charge}}{\text{Number of Metal Atoms}}$
components.interpretation: Calculate metal charge from formula: multiply anion charge by subscript, divide by metal subscript. Use result as Roman numeral in name.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Type II Metals from Formula
worked_examples.problem: For each compound, identify if the metal is Type II (variable charge): (a) FeCl₃, (b) NaCl, (c) CuO, (d) MgO, (e) PbO₂, (f) ZnCl₂.

step_by_step.calculation: Fe is transition metal (not Zn, Ag, Cd) → variable charge → Type II ✓
Na is Group 1 → always +1 → Type I ✗
Cu is transition metal (not Zn, Ag, Cd) → variable charge → Type II ✓
Mg is Group 2 → always +2 → Type I ✗
Pb is post-transition metal → variable charge (+2, +4) → Type II ✓
Zn is special transition metal → always +2 → Type I ✗

worked_examples.final_answer: Type II metals: (a) FeCl₃ ✓ (Fe, variable charge), (c) CuO ✓ (Cu, variable charge), (e) PbO₂ ✓ (Pb, variable charge). Type I metals: (b) NaCl ✗ (Na, Group 1), (d) MgO ✗ (Mg, Group 2), (f) ZnCl₂ ✗ (Zn, fixed +2). Type II = most transition metals + Pb, Sn, Bi, Hg.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying Type II vs. Type I metals from periodic position and exceptions

worked_examples.title: Calculating Metal Charge from Formula
worked_examples.problem: Determine the charge on the metal in each Type II compound: (a) FeCl₂, (b) FeCl₃, (c) Cu₂O, (d) PbO₂, (e) SnCl₄.

step_by_step.calculation: Cl is always -1; 2 Cl = -2 total; Fe must = +2 to balance → Fe²⁺
Cl is always -1; 3 Cl = -3 total; Fe must = +3 to balance → Fe³⁺
O is always -2; 1 O = -2 total; 2 Cu must = +2 total; each Cu = +1 → Cu⁺
O is always -2; 2 O = -4 total; Pb must = +4 to balance → Pb⁴⁺
Cl is always -1; 4 Cl = -4 total; Sn must = +4 to balance → Sn⁴⁺

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) FeCl₂: Fe²⁺ (2 Cl⁻ = -2, so Fe = +2). (b) FeCl₃: Fe³⁺ (3 Cl⁻ = -3, so Fe = +3). (c) Cu₂O: Cu⁺ (1 O²⁻ = -2, 2 Cu = +2, each Cu = +1). (d) PbO₂: Pb⁴⁺ (2 O²⁻ = -4, so Pb = +4). (e) SnCl₄: Sn⁴⁺ (4 Cl⁻ = -4, so Sn = +4). Charge calculation enables correct Roman numeral in name.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Calculating metal charge from anion charge and formula subscripts

sections.section_title: Naming Type II Binary Ionic Compounds
sections.introduction: Once you've identified a Type II metal and determined its charge, naming the compound follows a consistent pattern with the Roman numeral specifying the metal's charge. This distinguishes it from other compounds of the same elements.

key_concept.definition: Type II binary ionic compound naming: (1) Name the metal unchanged, (2) Add Roman numeral in parentheses indicating metal charge, (3) Name the nonmetal with -ide suffix. Example: FeCl₂ = iron(II) chloride.
key_concept.context: The Roman numeral is essential because the same metal can form different compounds with the same nonmetal. Without it, 'iron chloride' is ambiguous—could be FeCl₂ or FeCl₃.

classifications.behavior: Fe²⁺ (iron(II), ferrous) and Fe³⁺ (iron(III), ferric) form different compounds with different properties.

examples.explanation: Fe (iron) + charge +2 (II) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: iron(II) chloride. Formula: Fe²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → FeCl₂. Green color, reducing agent.

image.alt: FeCl₂ naming breakdown showing Roman numeral
image.caption: FeCl₂: iron(II) chloride (Fe²⁺, Roman numeral II specifies charge).

examples.explanation: Fe (iron) + charge +3 (III) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: iron(III) chloride. Formula: Fe³⁺ + 3Cl⁻ → FeCl₃. Yellow/brown color, oxidizing agent.

image.alt: FeCl₃ naming breakdown showing Roman numeral
image.caption: FeCl₃: iron(III) chloride (Fe³⁺, different compound from FeCl₂).

examples.explanation: Fe (iron) + charge +3 (III) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: iron(III) oxide. Formula: 2Fe³⁺ + 3O²⁻ → Fe₂O₃. Red-brown (rust color).

image.alt: Fe₂O₃ naming breakdown
image.caption: Fe₂O₃: iron(III) oxide (rust, Fe³⁺ with O²⁻).

classifications.behavior: Cu⁺ (copper(I), cuprous) and Cu²⁺ (copper(II), cupric) form different compounds; Cu²⁺ is more stable in aqueous solutions.

examples.explanation: Cu (copper) + charge +1 (I) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: copper(I) oxide. Formula: 2Cu⁺ + O²⁻ → Cu₂O. Red color, used in antifouling paint.

image.alt: Cu₂O naming breakdown showing Roman numeral I
image.caption: Cu₂O: copper(I) oxide (Cu⁺, red color).

examples.explanation: Cu (copper) + charge +2 (II) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: copper(II) oxide. Formula: Cu²⁺ + O²⁻ → CuO. Black color, more common.

image.alt: CuO naming breakdown showing Roman numeral II
image.caption: CuO: copper(II) oxide (Cu²⁺, black color, more common).

examples.explanation: Cu (copper) + charge +2 (II) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: copper(II) chloride. Formula: Cu²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → CuCl₂. Blue-green in solution.

image.alt: CuCl₂ naming breakdown
image.caption: CuCl₂: copper(II) chloride (Cu²⁺, blue-green solution).

classifications.behavior: Pb²⁺ (lead(II), plumbous) is more common; Pb⁴⁺ (lead(IV), plumbic) is strong oxidizing agent, found in car batteries.

examples.explanation: Pb (lead) + charge +2 (II) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: lead(II) oxide. Formula: Pb²⁺ + O²⁻ → PbO. Yellow color, used in ceramics.

image.alt: PbO naming breakdown showing Roman numeral II
image.caption: PbO: lead(II) oxide (Pb²⁺, yellow, more common).

examples.explanation: Pb (lead) + charge +4 (IV) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: lead(IV) oxide. Formula: Pb⁴⁺ + 2O²⁻ → PbO₂. Brown/black color, in car batteries.

image.alt: PbO₂ naming breakdown showing Roman numeral IV
image.caption: PbO₂: lead(IV) oxide (Pb⁴⁺, brown, in car batteries).

examples.explanation: Pb (lead) + charge +4 (IV) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: lead(IV) chloride. Formula: Pb⁴⁺ + 4Cl⁻ → PbCl₄. Unstable, decomposes to PbCl₂ + Cl₂.

image.alt: PbCl₄ naming breakdown
image.caption: PbCl₄: lead(IV) chloride (Pb⁴⁺, unstable).

classifications.behavior: Sn²⁺ (tin(II), stannous) is reducing agent; Sn⁴⁺ (tin(IV), stannic) is more stable oxidation state.

examples.explanation: Sn (tin) + charge +2 (II) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: tin(II) chloride. Formula: Sn²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → SnCl₂. White solid, reducing agent.

image.alt: SnCl₂ naming breakdown showing Roman numeral II
image.caption: SnCl₂: tin(II) chloride (Sn²⁺, reducing agent).

examples.explanation: Sn (tin) + charge +4 (IV) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: tin(IV) chloride. Formula: Sn⁴⁺ + 4Cl⁻ → SnCl₄. Fuming liquid, more stable.

image.alt: SnCl₄ naming breakdown showing Roman numeral IV
image.caption: SnCl₄: tin(IV) chloride (Sn⁴⁺, fuming liquid).

principles.statement: Roman numerals in Type II names follow specific format: parentheses around numeral, no space between metal and parenthesis, space after closing parenthesis, use Roman not Arabic numerals.
principles.application: Correct: iron(III) chloride, copper(II) oxide. Incorrect: iron III chloride (no parentheses), iron(3) chloride (Arabic), iron (III) chloride (space before parenthesis).
principles.example: FeCl₃: iron(III) chloride ✓ (correct format). FeCl₃: iron III chloride ✗ (missing parentheses). FeCl₃: iron(3) chloride ✗ (Arabic numeral).

image.alt: Correct and incorrect Roman numeral formatting
image.caption: Roman numeral format: metal(Roman numeral) nonmetal-ide, no spaces around numeral.

principles.statement: From a Type II name, write the formula by: (1) Identify metal and its charge from Roman numeral, (2) Identify nonmetal and its charge, (3) Balance charges to zero.
principles.application: Enables formula writing from name: 'copper(II) chloride' → Cu²⁺ + Cl⁻ → CuCl₂ (1(+2) + 2(-1) = 0).
principles.example: Name: lead(IV) oxide → Pb⁴⁺ + O²⁻ → PbO₂ (1(+4) + 2(-2) = 0). Roman numeral provides metal charge needed for formula.

image.alt: Flowchart showing Type II name-to-formula conversion
image.caption: Type II name to formula: Roman numeral gives metal charge, balance with anion charge.

principles.statement: From a Type II formula, write the name by: (1) Identify metal (Type II), (2) Calculate metal charge from anion, (3) Write name with Roman numeral, (4) Add nonmetal-ide.
principles.application: Enables naming from formula: 'CuO' → O²⁻ means Cu must be +2 → copper(II) oxide.
principles.example: Formula: Fe₂O₃ → 3 O²⁻ = -6, so 2 Fe = +6, each Fe = +3 → iron(III) oxide. Charge calculation determines Roman numeral.

image.alt: Flowchart showing Type II formula-to-name conversion
image.caption: Type II formula to name: calculate metal charge from anion, use as Roman numeral.

components.definition: Type II naming pattern with Roman numeral placement
components.interpretation: Metal name unchanged; Roman numeral in parentheses specifies charge; nonmetal root + -ide; Roman numeral = metal charge (I=1, II=2, III=3, IV=4, V=5, etc.).

worked_examples.title: Naming Type II Compounds from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following Type II ionic compounds: (a) FeS, (b) CuCl, (c) PbCl₄, (d) SnO, (e) Hg₂Cl₂.

step_by_step.calculation: Fe = iron (Type II); S = sulfide (S²⁻); Fe must be +2 to balance → iron(II) sulfide
Cu = copper (Type II); Cl = chloride (Cl⁻); Cu must be +1 to balance → copper(I) chloride
Pb = lead (Type II); Cl = chloride (Cl⁻); 4 Cl⁻ = -4, Pb must be +4 → lead(IV) chloride
Sn = tin (Type II); O = oxide (O²⁻); Sn must be +2 to balance → tin(II) oxide
Hg = mercury (Type II); Cl = chloride (Cl⁻); 2 Cl⁻ = -2, 2 Hg = +2, each Hg = +1 → mercury(I) chloride

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) FeS: iron(II) sulfide (Fe²⁺ + S²⁻). (b) CuCl: copper(I) chloride (Cu⁺ + Cl⁻). (c) PbCl₄: lead(IV) chloride (Pb⁴⁺ + 4Cl⁻). (d) SnO: tin(II) oxide (Sn²⁺ + O²⁻). (e) Hg₂Cl₂: mercury(I) chloride (Hg₂²⁺ + 2Cl⁻). All use Roman numerals to specify metal charge.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming Type II ionic compounds from formulas using charge calculation and Roman numerals

worked_examples.title: Writing Formulas from Type II Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for the following Type II ionic compounds: (a) iron(III) oxide, (b) copper(II) sulfide, (c) lead(II) chloride, (d) tin(IV) oxide, (e) mercury(II) chloride.

step_by_step.calculation: Fe³⁺ (from III) + O²⁻ → Need 2 Fe³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6) → Fe₂O₃
Cu²⁺ (from II) + S²⁻ → 1:1 ratio, charges balance → CuS
Pb²⁺ (from II) + Cl⁻ → Need 2 Cl⁻ to balance 1 Pb²⁺ → PbCl₂
Sn⁴⁺ (from IV) + O²⁻ → Need 2 O²⁻ to balance 1 Sn⁴⁺ → SnO₂
Hg²⁺ (from II) + Cl⁻ → Need 2 Cl⁻ to balance 1 Hg²⁺ → HgCl₂

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Fe₂O₃ (2 Fe³⁺ + 3 O²⁻ = 0). (b) CuS (Cu²⁺ + S²⁻ = 0). (c) PbCl₂ (Pb²⁺ + 2 Cl⁻ = 0). (d) SnO₂ (Sn⁴⁺ + 2 O²⁻ = 0). (e) HgCl₂ (Hg²⁺ + 2 Cl⁻ = 0). Roman numeral in name provides metal charge for formula writing.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing Type II ionic formulas from names using Roman numeral charge information

sections.introduction: Before the Stock system (Roman numerals), variable-charge metals were named using Latin roots with -ous and -ic suffixes. While IUPAC recommends Stock system, historical names persist in some contexts and you should recognize them.

key_concept.definition: Historical naming uses Latin metal roots with -ous suffix for lower charge and -ic suffix for higher charge. Example: Fe²⁺ = ferrous, Fe³⁺ = ferric. Stock system (iron(II), iron(III)) is preferred but historical names appear in older literature and some product names.
key_concept.context: The -ous/-ic system only distinguishes two common charges; Stock system can specify any charge. IUPAC recommends Stock system for clarity, but historical names persist in medicine, industry, and older textbooks.

classifications.behavior: -ous = lower common charge; -ic = higher common charge. Only works for metals with exactly two common charges.

examples.explanation: Fe²⁺: ferrous (Latin: ferrum + -ous); Fe³⁺: ferric (ferrum + -ic). Modern: iron(II), iron(III). Ferrous sulfate (FeSO₄) still used in supplements.

image.alt: Iron historical vs. modern naming comparison
image.caption: Iron: ferrous (Fe²⁺) = iron(II); ferric (Fe³⁺) = iron(III).

examples.explanation: Cu⁺: cuprous (Latin: cuprum + -ous); Cu²⁺: cupric (cuprum + -ic). Modern: copper(I), copper(II). Cupric sulfate (CuSO₄) common in labs.

image.alt: Copper historical vs. modern naming comparison
image.caption: Copper: cuprous (Cu⁺) = copper(I); cupric (Cu²⁺) = copper(II).

examples.explanation: Pb²⁺: plumbous (Latin: plumbum + -ous); Pb⁴⁺: plumbic (plumbum + -ic). Modern: lead(II), lead(IV). Plumbum is Latin for lead (Pb symbol).

image.alt: Lead historical vs. modern naming comparison
image.caption: Lead: plumbous (Pb²⁺) = lead(II); plumbic (Pb⁴⁺) = lead(IV).

examples.explanation: Sn²⁺: stannous (Latin: stannum + -ous); Sn⁴⁺: stannic (stannum + -ic). Modern: tin(II), tin(IV). Stannum is Latin for tin (Sn symbol).

image.alt: Tin historical vs. modern naming comparison
image.caption: Tin: stannous (Sn²⁺) = tin(II); stannic (Sn⁴⁺) = tin(IV).

examples.explanation: Hg₂²⁺: mercurous (Latin: hydrargyrum + -ous); Hg²⁺: mercuric (hydrargyrum + -ic). Modern: mercury(I), mercury(II). Hydrargyrum = 'liquid silver' (Hg symbol).

image.alt: Mercury historical vs. modern naming comparison
image.caption: Mercury: mercurous (Hg₂²⁺) = mercury(I); mercuric (Hg²⁺) = mercury(II).

principles.statement: To convert historical names to Stock system: -ous = lower charge (usually +2 or +1), -ic = higher charge (usually +3 or +4). Know the common charges for each metal.
principles.application: Ferrous chloride → Fe²⁺ → iron(II) chloride. Ferric chloride → Fe³⁺ → iron(III) chloride. Cupric sulfate → Cu²⁺ → copper(II) sulfate.
principles.example: Product label: 'Ferrous sulfate' → Fe²⁺ → iron(II) sulfate (FeSO₄). 'Ferric oxide' → Fe³⁺ → iron(III) oxide (Fe₂O₃, rust).

image.alt: Table converting historical names to Stock system
image.caption: Historical to Stock: -ous = lower charge (II), -ic = higher charge (III).

principles.statement: Historical names persist in: medicine (ferrous sulfate supplements), industry (cupric sulfate fungicide), older literature, and some product names.
principles.application: Recognize both systems: 'ferrous' in iron supplements means Fe²⁺; 'cupric' in algaecide means Cu²⁺. Stock system preferred in modern chemistry.
principles.example: Medicine: 'Ferrous gluconate' (Fe²⁺ supplement). Lab: 'Cupric sulfate pentahydrate' (CuSO₄·5H₂O). Both use historical names but Stock system is clearer.

image.alt: Examples of where historical names still appear
image.caption: Historical names persist in medicine, industry, and older literature.

components.interpretation: -ous = lower common charge (Fe²⁺, Cu⁺, Pb²⁺, Sn²⁺); -ic = higher common charge (Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Pb⁴⁺, Sn⁴⁺). Stock system preferred for clarity.

worked_examples.title: Converting Historical Names to Stock System
worked_examples.problem: Convert the following historical names to Stock system names and write formulas: (a) ferrous chloride, (b) ferric oxide, (c) cupric sulfate, (d) stannous fluoride, (e) plumbic oxide.

step_by_step.calculation: Ferrous = Fe²⁺ (lower charge) → iron(II) chloride → Fe²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → FeCl₂
Ferric = Fe³⁺ (higher charge) → iron(III) oxide → 2Fe³⁺ + 3O²⁻ → Fe₂O₃
Cupric = Cu²⁺ (higher charge) → copper(II) sulfate → Cu²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → CuSO₄
Stannous = Sn²⁺ (lower charge) → tin(II) fluoride → Sn²⁺ + 2F⁻ → SnF₂ (in toothpaste)
Plumbic = Pb⁴⁺ (higher charge) → lead(IV) oxide → Pb⁴⁺ + 2O²⁻ → PbO₂ (in batteries)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Ferrous chloride → iron(II) chloride, FeCl₂. (b) Ferric oxide → iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃. (c) Cupric sulfate → copper(II) sulfate, CuSO₄. (d) Stannous fluoride → tin(II) fluoride, SnF₂. (e) Plumbic oxide → lead(IV) oxide, PbO₂. Historical -ous = lower charge (II), -ic = higher charge (III or IV).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Converting historical -ous/-ic names to Stock system with Roman numerals

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Type II nomenclature has more opportunities for error than Type I due to the Roman numeral requirement and charge calculation. Recognizing these pitfalls and learning correction strategies builds accuracy and confidence.

key_concept.definition: Common Type II nomenclature errors include: omitting Roman numerals, using wrong Roman numeral, confusing Type I and Type II metals, incorrect charge calculation, and using Arabic instead of Roman numerals.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist prevents errors: Is the metal Type II? Roman numeral included? Correct charge calculated? Roman numeral format correct? Charges balance in formula?

classifications.value: Forgetting to include Roman numeral for variable-charge metals
characteristics: Writing 'iron chloride' instead of 'iron(II) chloride' or 'iron(III) chloride'
classifications.behavior: Results in ambiguous names; 'iron chloride' could be FeCl₂ or FeCl₃—completely different compounds with different properties.

examples.process: Error: Missing Roman numeral for iron
examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'iron chloride' for FeCl₂ or FeCl₃. Correct: 'iron(II) chloride' (FeCl₂) or 'iron(III) chloride' (FeCl₃). Roman numeral essential for Type II metals.

image.alt: Comparison showing need for Roman numerals in Type II
image.caption: Type II error: always include Roman numeral to specify metal charge.

examples.process: Error: Missing Roman numeral for copper
examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'copper oxide' for CuO or Cu₂O. Correct: 'copper(II) oxide' (CuO) or 'copper(I) oxide' (Cu₂O). Different compounds, different colors, different properties.

image.alt: Copper oxide naming error correction
image.caption: Copper oxide: must specify copper(I) or copper(II) with Roman numeral.

classifications.value: Calculating metal charge incorrectly, leading to wrong Roman numeral
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect names that describe different compounds: iron(II) chloride (FeCl₂) vs. iron(III) chloride (FeCl₃) are different substances.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'iron(II) chloride' for FeCl₃. Correct: 'iron(III) chloride'. 3 Cl⁻ = -3, so Fe must be +3 (III), not +2 (II).

image.alt: FeCl₃ charge calculation error
image.caption: FeCl₃: 3 Cl⁻ = -3, Fe must be +3 → iron(III), not iron(II).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'copper(II) oxide' for Cu₂O. Correct: 'copper(I) oxide'. 1 O²⁻ = -2, 2 Cu must = +2, each Cu = +1 (I), not +2 (II).

image.alt: Cu₂O charge calculation error
image.caption: Cu₂O: 2 Cu⁺ needed to balance O²⁻ → copper(I), not copper(II).

classifications.type: Confusing Type I and Type II Metals
classifications.value: Adding Roman numerals to Type I metals or omitting for Type II
characteristics: Writing 'sodium(I) chloride' (Type I doesn't need Roman numeral)
Writing 'iron chloride' without Roman numeral (Type II needs it)
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect names: Type I metals never use Roman numerals; Type II metals always do.

examples.process: Error: Roman numeral for Type I metal
examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'sodium(I) chloride' for NaCl. Correct: 'sodium chloride'. Na is always +1; Roman numeral unnecessary and wrong for Type I.

image.alt: Type I metal Roman numeral error
image.caption: Type I error: never use Roman numerals for fixed-charge metals like Na, Mg, Al.

examples.process: Error: No Roman numeral for Type II metal
examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'copper chloride' for CuCl₂. Correct: 'copper(II) chloride'. Cu is variable charge; Roman numeral required for Type II.

image.alt: Type II metal missing Roman numeral error
image.caption: Type II error: always use Roman numerals for variable-charge metals like Fe, Cu, Pb.

classifications.type: Using Arabic Instead of Roman Numerals
characteristics: Arabic numerals (1, 2, 3) instead of Roman (I, II, III)
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect format: IUPAC requires Roman numerals in parentheses, not Arabic numerals.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'iron(3) chloride' for FeCl₃. Correct: 'iron(III) chloride'. Use Roman numerals (I, II, III, IV, V...) not Arabic (1, 2, 3, 4, 5...).

image.alt: Arabic vs. Roman numeral error
image.caption: Type II naming: always use Roman numerals (I, II, III) not Arabic (1, 2, 3).

principles.statement: Always verify Type II names by checking: (1) Metal is Type II (variable charge), (2) Roman numeral included, (3) Roman numeral matches calculated charge, (4) Roman numeral format correct (parentheses, Roman not Arabic), (5) Charges balance in formula.
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit.
principles.example: Name: iron(III) chloride. Check: Fe is Type II ✓, Roman numeral included ✓, Fe³⁺ matches III ✓, format correct ✓, Fe³⁺ + 3Cl⁻ = 0 ✓. All checks pass.
principles.analogy: Like a pre-flight checklist: verify each item before considering the name/formula correct.

image.alt: Type II nomenclature verification checklist
image.caption: Type II checklist: Type II metal, Roman numeral included, correct charge, correct format, charge balance.

principles.statement: Always verify metal charge calculation: multiply anion charge by subscript, divide by metal subscript, result = Roman numeral.
principles.application: Prevents wrong Roman numeral errors; double-check calculation before writing name.
principles.example: Fe₂O₃: 3 O²⁻ = -6 total; 2 Fe must = +6 total; each Fe = +3 → iron(III) oxide. Verify: 2(+3) + 3(-2) = 0 ✓.

image.alt: Charge calculation verification steps
image.caption: Charge verification: calculate metal charge, verify formula balances to zero.

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each name or formula and provide the correct version: (a) 'iron chloride' for FeCl₃, (b) 'copper(2) oxide' for CuO, (c) 'sodium(II) chloride' for NaCl, (d) FeO for iron(III) oxide.

step_by_step.calculation: Error: missing Roman numeral for Type II metal. Correction: 3 Cl⁻ = -3, Fe = +3. Correct name: iron(III) chloride.
Error: Arabic numeral instead of Roman. Correction: Use Roman numeral II. Correct name: copper(II) oxide.
Error: Roman numeral for Type I metal. Correction: Na is always +1, no Roman numeral needed. Correct name: sodium chloride.
Error: formula doesn't match name. FeO: O²⁻ means Fe = +2 → iron(II) oxide. For iron(III) oxide: 2Fe³⁺ + 3O²⁻ → Fe₂O₃.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 'iron chloride' → iron(III) chloride (add Roman numeral; Fe is Type II). (b) 'copper(2) oxide' → copper(II) oxide (use Roman numeral II, not Arabic 2). (c) 'sodium(II) chloride' → sodium chloride (remove Roman numeral; Na is Type I). (d) FeO → Fe₂O₃ for iron(III) oxide (FeO is iron(II) oxide). Systematic verification catches these common Type II errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error identification and correction using Type II verification checklist

sections.introduction: Type II nomenclature mastery requires active practice with charge calculation and Roman numeral usage. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy with variable-charge metal naming and formula writing.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: naming from formulas (calculate charge, add Roman numeral), writing formulas from names (use Roman numeral for charge), identifying Type II metals, and correcting common errors.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce pattern recognition; focus on charge calculation accuracy and Roman numeral format.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Type II Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Complete the following: (1-5) Name these compounds: FeCl₂, CuO, PbO₂, SnCl₂, HgCl₂. (6-10) Write formulas for: iron(III) oxide, copper(I) sulfide, lead(II) chloride, tin(IV) fluoride, mercury(I) chloride.

step_by_step.calculation: Fe (Type II) + 2 Cl⁻ = -2, Fe = +2 → iron(II) chloride
Cu (Type II) + O²⁻ = -2, Cu = +2 → copper(II) oxide
Pb (Type II) + 2 O²⁻ = -4, Pb = +4 → lead(IV) oxide
Sn (Type II) + 2 Cl⁻ = -2, Sn = +2 → tin(II) chloride
Hg (Type II) + 2 Cl⁻ = -2, Hg = +2 → mercury(II) chloride
Fe³⁺ (from III) + O²⁻ → Need 2 Fe³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6) → Fe₂O₃
Cu⁺ (from I) + S²⁻ → Need 2 Cu⁺ to balance 1 S²⁻ → Cu₂S
Pb²⁺ (from II) + Cl⁻ → Need 2 Cl⁻ to balance 1 Pb²⁺ → PbCl₂
Sn⁴⁺ (from IV) + F⁻ → Need 4 F⁻ to balance 1 Sn⁴⁺ → SnF₄
Hg₂²⁺ (mercury(I) is diatomic) + 2 Cl⁻ → Hg₂Cl₂ (special case)

worked_examples.final_answer: Names: (1) iron(II) chloride, (2) copper(II) oxide, (3) lead(IV) oxide, (4) tin(II) chloride, (5) mercury(II) chloride. Formulas: (6) Fe₂O₃, (7) Cu₂S, (8) PbCl₂, (9) SnF₄, (10) Hg₂Cl₂. All Type II names include Roman numerals specifying metal charge; formulas from charge balance.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive Type II nomenclature practice covering naming and formula writing

worked_examples.title: Type I vs. Type II Identification Challenge
worked_examples.problem: For each compound, identify if Type I or Type II. If Type I, name without Roman numeral. If Type II, name with Roman numeral: (a) NaCl, (b) FeCl₂, (c) MgO, (d) CuO, (e) AlCl₃, (f) PbO, (g) ZnS, (h) SnCl₄.

step_by_step.calculation: Na is Group 1 → Type I → sodium chloride (no Roman numeral)
Fe is transition metal → Type II → Fe²⁺ → iron(II) chloride (Roman numeral required)
Mg is Group 2 → Type I → magnesium oxide (no Roman numeral)
Cu is transition metal → Type II → Cu²⁺ → copper(II) oxide (Roman numeral required)
Al is Group 13 → Type I → aluminum chloride (no Roman numeral)
Pb is post-transition → Type II → Pb²⁺ → lead(II) oxide (Roman numeral required)
Zn is special fixed-charge → Type I → zinc sulfide (no Roman numeral)
Sn is post-transition → Type II → Sn⁴⁺ → tin(IV) chloride (Roman numeral required)

worked_examples.final_answer: Type I (no Roman numeral): (a) NaCl → sodium chloride, (c) MgO → magnesium oxide, (e) AlCl₃ → aluminum chloride, (g) ZnS → zinc sulfide. Type II (with Roman numeral): (b) FeCl₂ → iron(II) chloride, (d) CuO → copper(II) oxide, (f) PbO → lead(II) oxide, (h) SnCl₄ → tin(IV) chloride. Type I = Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd; Type II = most transition metals + Pb, Sn, Bi, Hg.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Distinguishing Type I from Type II metals for correct nomenclature approach

summary: Type II binary ionic compounds contain variable-charge metals (most transition metals, Pb, Sn, Bi, Hg) that can form multiple different ions. Naming requires Roman numeral in parentheses to specify metal charge: [metal](Roman numeral)[nonmetal]-ide. Examples: FeCl₂ = iron(II) chloride (Fe²⁺), FeCl₃ = iron(III) chloride (Fe³⁺), CuO = copper(II) oxide (Cu²⁺), Cu₂O = copper(I) oxide (Cu⁺). Metal charge calculated from formula: multiply anion charge by subscript, divide by metal subscript. Roman numeral format: parentheses, Roman not Arabic, no space between metal and parenthesis. Historical names use -ous (lower charge) and -ic (higher charge): ferrous (Fe²⁺)/ferric (Fe³⁺), cuprous (Cu⁺)/cupric (Cu²⁺), plumbous (Pb²⁺)/plumbic (Pb⁴⁺), stannous (Sn²⁺)/stannic (Sn⁴⁺). Stock system (Roman numerals) preferred by IUPAC for clarity. Common errors: omitting Roman numerals (wrong for Type II), using wrong Roman numeral (charge calculation error), adding Roman numerals to Type I metals (wrong), using Arabic instead of Roman numerals (wrong format). Verification checklist: Type II metal? Roman numeral included? Correct charge? Correct format? Charges balance? Type I metals (Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd) never use Roman numerals; Type II metals (most transition + Pb, Sn, Bi, Hg) always do. Mastery of Type II naming essential for distinguishing different compounds of same elements (FeCl₂ vs. FeCl₃ are different substances).
